questions.question: An economy produces 500 units of Medicine or 2,000 units of Grain. A medical breakthrough doubles Medicine output to 1,000 while Grain stays at 2,000. What is the new opportunity cost of 1 unit of Medicine?
options: 4 units of Grain, the original opportunity cost before the medical breakthrough
2 units of Grain, the new ratio of maximum Grain to maximum Medicine output
0.5 units of Medicine per Grain, the inverse of the Grain-to-Medicine ratio now
1,000 units of Medicine, representing the total Medicine production capacity
questions.explanation: New opportunity cost of 1 Medicine = maximum Grain ÷ maximum Medicine = $2000 \div 1000 = 2$ units of Grain sacrificed per unit of Medicine.

questions.question: Home requires 10 hours for Laptops and 5 hours for Furniture. Foreign requires 15 hours for Laptops and 10 hours for Furniture. Which country has the absolute advantage in Furniture?
options: Foreign, because its Laptop and Furniture production times are closer together
Home, because it requires only 5 labor hours per unit of Furniture versus Foreign's 10 hours
Foreign, because its opportunity cost of Furniture is lower than Home's cost
Neither country, because each leads in a different product's labor time
questions.explanation: Home needs 5 hours per unit of Furniture vs. Foreign's 10 hours. Fewer labor hours means higher absolute productivity in Furniture.

questions.question: Using the data (Home: 10h/Laptop, 5h/Furniture; Foreign: 15h/Laptop, 10h/Furniture), what is Foreign's opportunity cost of 1 Laptop?
options: 0.67 units of Furniture, the inverse of Foreign's labor requirement ratio
1.5 units of Furniture, equal to Foreign's labor hours for Laptops divided by Furniture hours
10 units of Furniture, matching Foreign's total labor hours for Furniture production
15 units of Furniture, reflecting Foreign's total labor hours for Laptop production
questions.explanation: In Foreign, 1 Laptop takes 15 hours. In those 15 hours, Foreign could produce $15/10 = 1.5$ units of Furniture. The opportunity cost is 1.5 units of Furniture.

questions.question: An economy's Production Possibility Frontier is drawn as a perfectly straight line. What does this indicate?
options: Resources are highly specialized and cannot be moved between different industries
The marginal rate of transformation is constant regardless of the production mix
Opportunity costs increase sharply as the economy shifts toward producing one good
The economy is currently operating at a point located inside its production frontier
questions.explanation: A linear PPF means constant opportunity costs. The trade-off ratio between the two goods remains the same at every point along the frontier.

questions.question: In Ricardian autarky, $a_{LX} = 12$, $a_{LY} = 4$, and the nominal wage is $W = 30$. What is the relative price $P_Y/P_X$?
options: 3, calculated as the ratio of the larger to the smaller labor requirement
0.33, equal to the ratio of the unit labor requirements $a_{LY}/a_{LX}$
12, matching the absolute labor hours required to produce one unit of Good X
48, the product of both unit labor requirements multiplied together
questions.explanation: Autarky relative price $P_Y/P_X = a_{LY}/a_{LX} = 4/12 \approx 0.33$. One unit of Y costs approximately 0.33 units of X.

questions.question: The world relative price $P_X/P_Y$ is strictly between Home's and Foreign's opportunity costs. What is the global supply of Good X?
options: The combined output of both countries producing Good X
Only Home's total output of X, equal to $L / a_{LX}$, since Foreign specializes in Y
Only Foreign's total output of X, since Foreign has the comparative advantage in X
Zero, because neither country finds it profitable to produce X at that price
questions.explanation: Home has the comparative advantage in X and fully specializes in it. Foreign specializes entirely in Y. Only Home contributes to global X supply.

questions.question: The World Relative Demand curve for Good X shifts to the right in the Ricardian model. What happens to the equilibrium relative price?
options: The relative price always remains constant because supply is fixed in the Ricardian model
The relative price either stays the same on a horizontal step or increases to the next step
The relative price always decreases because higher demand attracts new producers into the market
The relative price fluctuates randomly and cannot be predicted from the demand shift
questions.explanation: Higher demand may push the equilibrium from a flat step (no price change) to a vertical segment or to the next, higher step of the RS curve. The price rises or stays the same.

questions.question: Home wage is 25, Foreign wage is 5. Home's $a_{LX} = 1$ and Foreign's $a^*_{LX} = 8$. Which country will produce Good X?
options: Foreign, because its absolute wage level is much lower than Home's wage rate
Home, because its unit labor cost of 25 is lower than Foreign's unit cost of 40
Both countries will produce the good in proportion to their relative labor forces
Neither country, because absolute advantage cannot determine production location
questions.explanation: Home unit labor cost = $25 \times 1 = 25$. Foreign unit labor cost = $5 \times 8 = 40$. Home is the lower-cost producer and will produce Good X.

questions.question: Why do most economists consider the sweatshop labor argument against free trade to be flawed?
options: Sweatshops do not actually exist in any country that participates in international trade
It ignores the fact that low wages in developing countries primarily reflect low productivity levels
International law requires all countries to adopt identical minimum wage laws and standards
Trade always benefits high-wage countries at the expense of low-wage trading partners
questions.explanation: Low wages reflect low productivity, not exploitation. Trade increases demand for labor in developing countries, gradually raising real wages and living standards over time.

questions.question: Why is trade described as a vent for surplus in the Ricardian model?
options: Trade allows a country to dispose of goods it cannot sell in its domestic market at any price
Specialization creates output beyond domestic demand, which can be exchanged for needed imports
The term refers to government programs that subsidize the disposal of surplus production
Vent for surplus is a concept from the H-O model, not the Ricardian model of trade
questions.explanation: A specialized economy produces more of its export good than it can consume domestically. Trade provides an outlet for this surplus, exchanging it for goods the country needs.

questions.question: A country's terms of trade improve. What is the effect on its national welfare?
options: National welfare decreases because improved terms of trade mean the country is exporting more
National welfare increases because the country receives more imports for the same volume of exports
National welfare remains unchanged because terms of trade only affect the price ratio, not real income
National welfare increases only if the country also runs a Current Account surplus
questions.explanation: An improvement in the terms of trade means export prices rise relative to import prices. The country can buy more imports with each unit of exports, increasing real national income and welfare.

questions.question: A move to free trade makes 90% of the population significantly richer and 10% slightly poorer. Is this change a Pareto Improvement?
options: Yes, because the total gains to the 90% clearly outweigh the small losses to the 10%
No, because a Pareto Improvement requires that absolutely no individual is made worse off
Yes, because the vast majority of the population benefits from the trade policy change
No, but only if the 10% who lost are in the same industry or geographic region
questions.explanation: The strict Pareto criterion requires that no one, not even a single individual, suffers any loss. The 10% who are worse off disqualify it from being a Pareto Improvement.

questions.question: Which policy is most likely to transform free trade from a potential Pareto Improvement into an actual one?
options: Imposing additional tariffs to protect all import-competing domestic industries
Providing trade adjustment assistance and retraining grants to workers displaced by imports
Eliminating all government involvement and letting market forces operate freely
Negotiating voluntary export restraints with the principal foreign supplying countries
questions.explanation: By using gains from winners to compensate and retrain losers, trade adjustment assistance can help ensure that no one ends up worse off, achieving an actual Pareto Improvement.

questions.question: In the Ricardian model with perfectly mobile labor, what must be true about nominal wages across sectors in equilibrium?
options: Wages will differ permanently, reflecting each sector's different labor productivity
Wages must be equal across all sectors actively producing within the domestic economy
The export sector always pays a higher wage than the import-competing sector
The government must set a uniform national wage for all industries to achieve equilibrium
questions.explanation: With perfect mobility, any wage differential would trigger immediate migration of workers. Equilibrium requires equal wages in all actively producing sectors.

questions.question: A country has $a_{LX} = 5$, $a_{LY} = 10$, and world prices are $P_X = 20$, $P_Y = 60$. Where should the country allocate all its labor?
options: Equally between the two sectors because both offer positive returns to labor
Entirely to Sector Y, where the wage of 6 exceeds Sector X's wage of only 4
Entirely to Sector X, because it has the lower unit labor requirement of the two sectors
The allocation is indeterminate because wages are equal in both sectors at these prices
questions.explanation: $W_X = 20/5 = 4$. $W_Y = 60/10 = 6$. Rational workers move entirely to the higher-paying Sector Y until X production ceases.

questions.question: A natural resource boom causes Dutch Disease in an economy. What is the economic mechanism at work?
options: The resource boom raises taxes, which the government uses to subsidize all other sectors
Higher wages in the booming resource sector draw mobile labor away from manufacturing and agriculture
Foreign demand for the resource causes the domestic currency to depreciate, helping other exports
The resource sector creates external economies that benefit all other industries in the country
questions.explanation: The booming sector bids up wages, pulling labor away from other tradable sectors like manufacturing. These sectors contract, a phenomenon known as Dutch Disease or the Resource Curse.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, the government provides a subsidy to the export sector. What happens to nominal incomes in the import-competing sector?
options: Nominal incomes rise because the booming export sector lifts wages for all workers
Nominal incomes see no change because immobile factors cannot move to the subsidized sector
Nominal incomes fall because the subsidy draws government resources away from the import sector
Nominal incomes rise in the short run but fall back in the long run as the subsidy expires
questions.explanation: Factors are trapped in the import-competing sector. They cannot relocate to the subsidized export sector to benefit from higher returns. Their nominal income remains unchanged.

questions.question: Why does the immobile factor model predict stronger political opposition to trade liberalization than the Ricardian model?
options: Because trade benefits are larger in the immobile model, creating more intense political conflict
It identifies specific groups who face permanent income losses without any ability to transition out
The Ricardian model assumes that all workers always lose from trade in every possible scenario
Immobile factor models predict that no one ever benefits from trade liberalization at all
questions.explanation: In the Ricardian model, labor can move to the expanding sector. In the immobile model, those in the contracting sector are trapped with permanent losses, creating intense and concentrated opposition.

questions.question: In the immobile factor model, which group benefits from the opening of international trade?
options: All factors of production benefit equally because trade expands the total size of the economy
Factors of production that are specific to and employed in the export-oriented industries
Factors in the import-competing sector, because they gain access to cheaper imported inputs
No one benefits in the immobile factor model; trade always reduces total national welfare
questions.explanation: Export prices rise with trade, increasing nominal returns in that sector. Since other factors cannot enter, those specific to the export sector capture the full benefit of the price increase.

questions.question: Country A has $K = 1000$ and $L = 5000$. Country B has $K = 500$ and $L = 1000$. According to H-O definitions, which country is labor-abundant?
options: Country B, because it has a smaller absolute number of workers in its total economy
Country A, because its L/K ratio of 5 exceeds Country B's L/K ratio of 2
Country B, because a smaller total economy is always relatively more labor-abundant
Neither country, because both have more labor than capital in absolute terms
questions.explanation: Country A L/K = $5000/1000 = 5$. Country B L/K = $1000/500 = 2$. Country A has more labor per unit of capital and is relatively labor-abundant.

questions.question: Under what condition does the Factor Price Equalization theorem hold in the H-O model?
options: One country must fully specialize in producing only its comparative advantage good
Both countries must continue to produce both goods after trade has been opened
Factor Price Equalization holds regardless of whether countries fully specialize or not
The theorem requires that labor be internationally mobile, not just goods trade
questions.explanation: FPE relies on the link between goods prices and factor prices. If a country fully specializes, this link is broken and factor prices no longer equalize across countries.

questions.question: The US is capital-abundant and Mexico is labor-abundant. According to the H-O model, what happens to the wage-to-rental ratio in Mexico when trade opens?
options: The wage-to-rental ratio falls because capital becomes relatively more valuable
The wage-to-rental ratio increases because demand for Mexico's labor-intensive exports rises
The wage-to-rental ratio remains constant because factor endowments are fixed in the model
The ratio converges downward to match the US wage-to-rental ratio through equalization
questions.explanation: Trade expands the labor-intensive sector in the labor-abundant country. Increased derived demand for labor raises its relative price, increasing $w/r$ in Mexico.

questions.question: According to the Rybczynski Theorem, an increase in a country's labor supply causes what change in output composition?
options: Output of both goods increases proportionally because the entire economy expands
Output of the labor-intensive good expands more than proportionally while the capital-intensive good contracts
Only the capital-intensive sector expands because labor is now relatively cheaper to employ
The output mix remains unchanged because goods prices, not endowments, determine production
questions.explanation: The expanding labor-intensive sector draws capital away from the other sector. Labor-intensive output rises more than proportionally, while capital-intensive output actually declines.

questions.question: Capital is specific to Manufacturing and Land to Food. $P_{Manufacturing}$ rises by 10%. What happens to the return to Manufacturing Capital?
options: The return rises by exactly 10%, matching the price increase in the manufacturing sector
The return rises by more than 10% due to the Magnification Effect for specific factors
The return remains constant because capital is fixed and cannot respond to price changes
The return initially rises but falls back as labor moves into the manufacturing sector
questions.explanation: The Magnification Effect for specific factors means the return to the specific factor in the sector with the price increase changes more than proportionally. Capital's return rises by more than 10%.

questions.question: The total supply of mobile labor increases in the Specific Factor Model. What happens to the output of the two sectors?
options: Output falls in both sectors because more workers dilute the available capital and land
Output increases in both sectors as the now cheaper labor is distributed across both industries
Output rises only in the labor-intensive sector while the capital-intensive sector contracts
Output remains unchanged because specific factors limit total production capacity
questions.explanation: More labor means both fixed-factor sectors can employ additional workers at the lower equilibrium wage. Total output expands in both Manufacturing and Food production.

questions.question: In the Specific Factor Model, the import-competing sector contracts due to trade. What happens to the return of its specific factor?
options: The return rises because the factor becomes relatively scarcer as the sector shrinks
The return falls in both nominal and real terms as demand and prices in that sector decline
The nominal return falls but the real return rises because import prices are now lower
The return remains constant because specific factors are immobile and fixed in supply
questions.explanation: The contracting sector's specific factor faces lower demand for its services and a lower output price due to import competition. Both its nominal and real returns decline.

questions.question: What shape does the marginal product of labor curve have in the Specific Factor Model?
options: It is perfectly horizontal, reflecting constant returns to the mobile factor labor
It slopes downward, reflecting the Law of Diminishing Marginal Returns to the mobile factor
It slopes upward because more labor makes the specific factors more productive
It is perfectly vertical because the quantity of labor is fixed in the short run
questions.explanation: As more labor is added to a fixed amount of the specific factor (Capital or Land), each additional worker adds less to total output. The MPL curve slopes downward.

questions.question: Under internal economies of scale, what does international trade allow consumers to enjoy?
options: Lower prices only, but with a much more limited variety of products to choose from
Both lower prices and a wider selection of differentiated product varieties
Higher prices but with improved product quality and after-sales service
The same prices and variety as under autarky because trade does not affect these outcomes
questions.explanation: The larger integrated market allows firms to produce at higher scale (lower average costs and prices) while supporting more differentiated firms than either country could alone.

questions.question: External economies of scale occur when a firm's production costs depend on which factor?
options: The firm's own individual production volume, with larger firms having lower costs
The total size of the entire industry in that location, regardless of the individual firm's size
The exchange rate between the domestic currency and foreign trading partner currencies
Government subsidies that reduce costs for all firms operating in any industry
questions.explanation: External economies arise from the overall scale of the industry in a geographic area. Benefits include specialized labor pools, supplier networks, and knowledge spillovers shared by all firms.

questions.question: Intra-industry trade is most commonly observed in which type of product category?
options: Homogeneous raw materials like crude oil and iron ore between nations
Highly differentiated manufactured goods like passenger automobiles and electronics
Agricultural commodities that are identical regardless of their country of origin
Intra-industry trade does not occur in any real-world product categories
questions.explanation: Differentiated products like automobiles exhibit strong intra-industry trade. Countries both export and import different brands within the same industry, driven by consumer preferences for variety.

questions.question: What is trade diversion in the context of forming a Customs Union?
options: Creating entirely new trade flows between member countries that did not previously exist
A member country switching imports from a more efficient non-member to a less efficient bloc partner
Redirecting domestic production away from exports and toward goods for local consumption
The relocation of manufacturing facilities from one member country to another within the union
questions.explanation: Trade diversion is welfare-reducing. Internal tariff elimination makes a member's goods artificially cheaper than a more efficient non-member's goods, which still face the common external tariff.

questions.question: What is the specific purpose of a countervailing duty imposed on imported goods?
options: To raise general government revenue from all categories of imported merchandise
To offset the competitive price advantage created by a foreign government's export subsidy
To punish foreign countries that refuse to join the World Trade Organization
To encourage domestic firms to increase their own exports to international markets
questions.explanation: Countervailing duties are WTO-permitted tariffs specifically designed to neutralize the artificial price advantage foreign firms receive from their government's financial support.

questions.question: Which of the following is classified as a non-tariff barrier to international trade?
options: A 15% ad valorem tax calculated on the assessed customs value of the imported good
Stringent sanitary and phytosanitary regulations that impose costly compliance on foreign producers
A fixed dollar amount charged for each physical unit of the imported product
A combination of both a specific charge and a percentage-based tax on the same good
questions.explanation: SPS regulations are technical rules that, even when scientifically justified, can function as trade barriers by being more burdensome for foreign firms to satisfy than domestic ones.

questions.question: At what point is the optimal tariff rate for a large country achieved?
options: When the tariff is set high enough to completely eliminate all imports of the protected good
When the marginal terms-of-trade gain equals the marginal deadweight loss from the tariff
When the tariff generates the maximum possible amount of government revenue from imports
Zero, because any positive tariff always reduces the national welfare of large countries
questions.explanation: The optimal tariff maximizes national welfare by balancing the benefit of improved terms of trade (lower world prices) against the cost of domestic efficiency distortions.

questions.question: A small country has domestic supply $Q_S = P$ and demand $Q_D = 100 - P$. The world price is 20. A tariff of 10 is imposed. What is the total deadweight loss?
options: 50, representing only the production distortion from the tariff imposition
100, the sum of the production and consumption distortion triangles combined
200, reflecting the total reduction in imports caused by the tariff
400, the product of the tariff
